key_points: Singles nearly impossible — reserved for students with disabilities only
Assumption Hall preferred over Towers; recently renovated and smaller floors
Off-campus options in Southside, Shadyside popular; much cheaper than on-campus
key_quotes: Singles are not an option at Towers
Assumption is a million times better than towers and was recently updated
S-level is indeed the basement, I lived there my freshman year

key_points: Campus feels insular and homogeneous — described as 'high school extended'
Alt/punk/metal scene exists in Pittsburgh but nothing like that on campus
Party scene exists but requires actively seeking it out; frats post sporadically
key_quotes: For me, attending Duquesne felt like an extension the high school experience, but maybe worse
Definitely a scene nearby, but nothing like that at all on campus unfortunately
Duquesne's demographics felt like being back in high school

key_points: Hogan dining hall closed for renovations; reopening timeline unclear
Chick-fil-A lines notoriously long — students avoid it most days
Flex dollars run out fast; prioritize plans with more flex over meal swipes
key_quotes: I ran out of meal swipes in the first sem so it was a lit...
I always went to Cinco. I ate at Chick-fil-A maybe twice per semester because of lines
Get the one with the most flex. You'll go thru it way faster than meal swipes

key_points: LGBTQ+ students report feeling isolated; few non-conformists on campus
High dropout rate among freshmen noted by multiple students
No direct mentions of counseling services quality in student data
key_quotes: In my exp, most atheists/LGBTQ keep it to themselves
A lot of freshmen drop out after their first...
My rural high school at least had a nice collection of non-conformists

key_points: Merit scholarships arrive ~2 weeks after acceptance; full rides exist but rare
Even full-ride students still owe ~$15K/year for room and board
Financial aid portal (DORI) confusing — many students can't locate their package
key_quotes: I was awarded $56,000 a year for a full ride scholarship. I still pay ~15K a year for room and board
We just received merit. Sadly it was not enough to go there
I received a merit scholarship like a week after receiving my initial acceptance

key_points: Small class sizes and accessible professors are the strongest academic selling point
Humanities are 'not the school's strong suit'; IR/ISS department underrated
Pharmacy and nursing programs well-regarded; engineering considered weak
key_quotes: Classes are small and professors remember you
The humanities aren't the school's strong suit, but it's not bad by any means
The IR/ISS part of the school is very slept on and the classes taught are fantastic

key_points: Financial aid office processes are slow and portal navigation is genuinely confusing
Housing office is strict on singles; disability-only policy surprises many families
Two-week commit deadlines for special programs flagged as pressure tactics
key_quotes: Singles are not an option at Towers... single dorms are limited to students with disabilities only
We spoke to his admissions officer and she said to just ignore it. It's a marketing thing
Financial aid would be one of the only offices with this answer, as they'll release it in waves

key_points: Campus sits on 'the Bluff' — feels self-contained despite being in a major city
Pittsburgh itself is vibrant, LGBTQ-friendly, and accessible via free shuttle to Oakland
CMU and Pitt are 10-15 minutes away by bus; cross-library access not yet available
key_quotes: It's a college campus in a major city but it doesn't feel like you're in a major city
Pittsburgh itself is very lgbtq friendly
CMU is literally just a mile away from Duq

key_points: Pre-pharmacy guarantee only applies to students who start at Duquesne, not transfers
MBS-to-DO pathway has ~50% admission rate; MCAT 500 strictly enforced
No strong data on general career placement; school name carries regional recognition
key_quotes: Normally if you start here, you can be a 'pre pharmacy' major and be a guaranteed 'in' to the pharmacy school. However, I didn't begin here
The MCAT score is non-negotiable
Duquesne is a generally well-regarded R2 Catholic university

key_points: Sticker price is high; merit aid widely available but rarely covers full cost
Multiple students chose not to attend solely due to cost after aid
If planning grad school, peer advice is unanimous: go cheaper elsewhere
key_quotes: If your plan is grad school then cheapest undergrad possible
Merit was sizable but the cost was just too high
I got ~$30,000 a year, which I think is about average

key_points: Self-motivated students thrive; passive students likely to struggle or drop out
Catholic identity is visible everywhere but enforcement is moderate in practice
Best described as a small, tight-knit campus embedded in a great city
key_quotes: If you like school and learning, and are at least semi motivated, you'll be fine
There is a crucifix in every classroom, and all over
It's what you make of it imo

key_points: **Diversity & Inclusion:** one student calls it 'worst school for minorities, don't even consider it'
**Dropout Rate:** multiple students note high freshman attrition with no explanation offered
**Library Resources:** student flagged 'terrible shortages of books' and no cross-library access with Pitt/CMU
key_quotes: Worst school for minorities. Don't even consider it if you are a minority
A lot of freshmen drop out after their first...
Duq have terrible shortages of books unfortunately

key_points: Power Center gym is genuinely excellent — graduates say they miss it after leaving
Graduate health insurance covers birth control at $0 copay despite Catholic affiliation
Southside free shuttle makes car-free living very viable for off-campus students
key_quotes: Gym is great. I graduated last year and now go to a Crunch Fitness and I really miss the Duquesne gym
With the school's insurance it is $0 — I was considering turning down my offer over this rumor
Southside is a good option because there's also a free shuttle that takes you to campus
